--- SOURCE 2: Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a ... ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1

SUMMARY:
Human-robot collaboration (HRC) introduces significant safety challenges, particularly in protecting human operators working alongside

FULL CONTENT:
# Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a Heuristics-based Approach

###### Abstract

Human-robot collaboration (HRC) introduces significant safety challenges, particularly in protecting human operators working alongside collaborative robots (cobots). While current ISO standards emphasize risk assessment and hazard identification, these procedures are
often insufficient for addressing the complexity of HRC environments, which involve numerous design factors and dynamic interactions. This publication presents a method for objective hazard analysis to support Dynamic Risk Assessment, extending beyond reliance on expert knowledge. The approach monitors scene parameters, such as the distance between human body parts and the cobot, as well as the cobot’s Cartesian velocity. Additionally, an anthropocentric parameter focusing on the orientation of the human head within the collaborative workspace is introduced. These parameters are transformed into hazard indicators using non-linear heuristic functions. The hazard indicators are then aggregated to estimate the total hazard level of a given scenario. The proposed method is evaluated using an industrial dataset that depicts various interactions between a human operator and a cobot.

###### keywords:

## 1 Introduction

Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) is increasingly being deployed in industrial contexts. This trend is underscored by the projected growth of the global collaborative robot (cobot) market, with its size expected to increase exponentially by 2030203020302030 (Next Move Strategy Consulting, [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib13)). In HRC, cobots and human operators share a workspace, combining the strengths of robots—such as the ability to perform repetitive tasks with high precision and efficiency—with the advanced cognitive and sensory capabilities of humans (ISO 15066, [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib8)). The emergence of HRC introduces not only opportunities but also a range of challenges. The most critical challenge is ensuring the safety of human operators. To address this issue, it is necessary to conduct a thorough risk assessment prior to the implementation of a robot system. This assessment should evaluate the robot itself, as well as the work environment (ISO 10218-2, [2011](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib7)). Additionally, collaborative systems need to employ sophisticated methodologies, such as adaptive speed limitations, area monitoring, power and force limitation to achieve safety requirements set forth in the standards (ISO 15066, [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib8)).

Nevertheless, a lot of challenges regarding safety remain unaddressed.
Huck et al. ([2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib6)) argue that risk assessment as recommended by current standards, relying on experience and expert knowledge, is challenging to apply to HRC. The difficulty arises due to the fields inherent complexity, the limited experience in this domain, challenges in predicting human behavior, and the intricacies of estimating collision criticality.
Among these, lack of experience is often cited as the most significant barrier. Practitioners frequently report insufficient knowledge of various aspects of the domain (Aaltonen and Salmi, [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib1)). Moreover, questions arise regarding which operational safety functions and methods should be applied at different interaction levels in HRC, and which robot parameters should be measured or controlled during each level of interaction (Bdiwi et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib2)). Finally, Zacharaki et al. ([2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib15)) address the need for mathematical modeling and systematization of existing State-of-the-Art methods in order to allow hazard avoidance prior to accidents. They contend that the establishment of unified metrics and datasets is paramount to enhance safety.

Addressing the identified shortcomings requires a Dynamic Risk Assessment (DRA) method that extends beyond sole reliance on expert knowledge. This paper proposes an objective approach to hazard identification, wherein the hazard level in a given a scenario is dynamically quantified through numerical values. Hazard analysis—a key aspect of risk assessment—is conducted by defining hazard indicators that are based on mathematical functions. This ensures a consistent and objective evaluation of a scenario’s danger level and severity. By providing a structured, data-driven understanding of hazards, the method aims to simplify subsequent risk estimation for system integrators or robotic systems.

The main contributions are twofold. Firstly, an anthropocentric parameter based on human head orientation is introduced. Secondly, based on scene parameters and the anthropocentric parameter, non-linear, heuristic hazard indicators are developed. These indicators are then aggregated into a comprehensive framework for quantifying and assessing hazards, enabling a more consistent and systematic scenario analysis that incorporates human behavior.

## 2 Related Work

ISO/TR 14121−214121214121\mathrm{-}214121 - 2 provides practical methods for implementing an ISO 12100121001210012100-compliant risk assessment process, defining risk indices based on the probability of hazardous events and the severity of potential accidents. While the standard outlines quantitative and semi-quantitative approaches, its methods for calculating risk indices include subjective elements. Initial system assumptions are evaluated using risk graphs, a risk matrix, or numerical scoring, assigning values to harm severity and occurrence probability. Numerical scoring uses numbers, whereas other methods rely on descriptive categories like ”very likely” or ”rare” to characterize risk factors. The assigned values are then combined to produce a total risk score (ISO/TR 14121-2, [2012](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib9)). Kulić and Croft ([2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib11)) formulated a numerical danger index to evaluate human-robot interaction risks. The index incorporates parameters such as the distance between robot and human, relative velocity, and effective robot inertia at impact points. These parameters collectively capture the likelihood and severity of a potential collision, computing risk for the closest point on each robot link to the human. Sanderud et al. ([2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib14)) proposed the creation of a dynamic risk map of the workspace, which incorporates both the likelihood of human presence and the severity of potential consequences. Their model dynamically adjusts based on limb velocity, along with other parameters such as proximity and body part criticality for evaluating risks. Similarly, Dittrich and Woern ([2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib4)) developed a Bayesian Network (BN)-based model for dynamic risk estimation. Their model combines local body-part-specific information with global situational factors, such as human attention or posture, using Fuzzy Logic (FL). Another study by Beltran et al. ([2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib3)) combined FL, Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS) to compute a Hazard Rating Number. Their model evaluates factors like proximity, speeds, head orientation, and upper body posture. Finally, Huck et al. ([2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib5)) presents a simplified heuristic metric to identify hazard. In their publication, a deterministic function to compute the risk is defined based on the proximity between human and robot in the workspace.

## 3 Heuristics-based Hazard Analysis

### 3.1 Quantitative Definition of Hazards and Risk

To formally quantify risks and hazards this work adopts the definition proposed by Kaplan and Garrick ([1981](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib10)) who define risk as a set of triplets representing all possible risky scenarios:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | R={⟨si,pi,xi⟩}𝑅  subscript𝑠isubscript𝑝isubscript𝑥iR=\{\langle s\_{\mathrm{i}},p\_{\mathrm{i}},x\_{\mathrm{i}}\rangle\}italic\_R = { ⟨ italic\_s start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_p start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⟩ } |  | (1) |

where sisubscript𝑠is\_{\mathrm{i}}italic\_s start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT is a scenario identification or description, pisubscript𝑝ip\_{\mathrm{i}}italic\_p start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the probability of that scenario, and xisubscript𝑥ix\_{\mathrm{i}}italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the consequence or evaluation measure of
that scenario. Naturally, a formal definition of hazard can be defined as:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | H={⟨si,pi⟩}𝐻  subscript𝑠isubscript𝑝iH=\{\langle s\_{\mathrm{i}},p\_{\mathrm{i}}\rangle\}italic\_H = { ⟨ italic\_s start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_p start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⟩ } |  | (2) |

This definition serves as the basis for the adopted methodology, with two reinterpretations. First, a scenario is treated as a collection of measurable parameters that assess the associated danger. For example, the velocity of a robot can either pose a hazard or remain harmless. In this context, probabilities are not computed for the existence of a dangerous scenario. Instead, a continuous numerical characterization of the danger is undertaken based on the identified parameters, resulting in hazard indicator values. Existing standards aim to create inherently safe systems by defining acceptable parameter ranges for robots to operate within human-compatible limits. Leveraging this fact allows for precise hazard identification within specific velocity ranges. This principle extends to other parameters, such as distance, enabling direct hazard identification without relying on probabilistic estimates. Additionally, incorporating human behavior introduces variability, as actions within the workspace can transform an otherwise safe scenario into a hazardous one.

### 3.2 Heuristic Metrics

To analyze hazards effectively, relevant system parameters must first be identified and made comparable. The proposed method for quantifying a scenario as hazardous is based on heuristic functions. It is inspired by the works of Huck et al. ([2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib5)) and Kulić and Croft ([2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib11)). The identified parameters are then mapped from their domain to a range of [0,1]01[0,1][ 0 , 1 ] through the heuristic functions. These are non-linear, introducing a skew that emphasizes high-danger scenarios by increasing sensitivity near maximum thresholds and reducing it near minimum values. This framework offers several advantages. It provides a transparent, deterministic method for hazard identification, ensuring all parameters contribute clearly and are adjustable. Unlike machine learning-based models, this approach does not require large annotated datasets, making it ideal for scenarios where such data is unavailable or impractical to obtain. Furthermore, it is highly flexible, allowing for quick adaptation to specific tasks or environmental changes.

To determine the required functions, the parameters to be monitored must first be established. ISO 15066 ([2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib8)) identifies essential parameters, including separation distance between human and robot, and robot velocity. Additionally, a new anthropocentric parameter, the Position of Human Head (PHH), is introduced to account for human awareness. The standard emphasizes, that the head and face are particularly vulnerable areas of the human body. Given this sensitivity, it is critical to closely track the position of the human head during operations. Head movement can provide significant insights into potentially hazardous scenarios that would otherwise be safe if a person was attentive.

### 3.3 Definition of Hazard Indicators

#### 3.3.1 Distance-based Hazard Indicator

To identify the hazard level of a scenario based on the proximity between a robot and a human, the indicator must reflect that the indicator of harm increases as the distance decreases. Conversely, greater distances reduce the associated indicator. Huck et al. ([2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib5)) model this relationship using a heuristic negative exponential function, which serves as the foundation for the proposed approach.

The baseline function is augmented with parameters to account for robot-specific constraints and established safety standards. The function is expressed as:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | rD⁢(dH)={e−α⋅(dH−dmin),if ⁢dmin≤dH<dreach,0,if ⁢dH≥dreach,1,if ⁢dH≤dminsubscript𝑟Dsubscript𝑑Hcasessuperscript𝑒⋅𝛼subscript𝑑Hsubscript𝑑minif subscript𝑑minsubscript𝑑Hsubscript𝑑reach0if subscript𝑑Hsubscript𝑑reach1if subscript𝑑Hsubscript𝑑minr\_{\mathrm{D}}(d\_{\mathrm{H}})=\begin{cases}e^{-\alpha\cdot(d\_{\mathrm{H}}-d\_{% \mathrm{min}})},&\text{if }d\_{\mathrm{min}}\leq d\_{\mathrm{H}}<d\_{\mathrm{% reach}},\\ 0,&\text{if }d\_{\mathrm{H}}\geq d\_{\mathrm{reach}},\\ 1,&\text{if }d\_{\mathrm{H}}\leq d\_{\mathrm{min}}\end{cases}italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_D end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = { start\_ROW start\_CELL italic\_e start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - italic\_α ⋅ ( italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , end\_CELL start\_CELL if italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≤ italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_reach end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , end\_CELL end\_ROW start\_ROW start\_CELL 0 , end\_CELL start\_CELL if italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≥ italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_reach end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , end\_CELL end\_ROW start\_ROW start\_CELL 1 , end\_CELL start\_CELL if italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≤ italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT end\_CELL end\_ROW |  | (3) |

Parameter α𝛼\alphaitalic\_α controls the steepness of the curve, dHsubscript𝑑Hd\_{\mathrm{H}}italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the distance between the human and the robot, and dminsubscript𝑑mind\_{\mathrm{min}}italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT denotes the minimum safety distance as specified in ISO/TS 15066:2016.
An additional constraint includes the robot’s maximum reach, dreachsubscript𝑑reachd\_{\mathrm{reach}}italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_reach end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT, which defines the physical limit of its workspace.

The parameter dminsubscript𝑑mind\_{\mathrm{min}}italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ensures that rD⁢(dmin)=1subscript𝑟Dsubscript𝑑min1r\_{\mathrm{D}}(d\_{\mathrm{min}})=1italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_D end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = 1 when the human is within the minimum safe distance from the robot. It can be determined by using the robot’s stopping time, Tstopsubscript𝑇stopT\_{\mathrm{stop}}italic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_stop end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT, for a given velocity through the inequality: (ISO 15066, [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib8))

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | dmin≥v⋅Tstopsubscript𝑑min⋅𝑣subscript𝑇stopd\_{\mathrm{min}}\geq v\cdot T\_{\mathrm{stop}}italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≥ italic\_v ⋅ italic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_stop end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT |  | (4) |

dreachsubscript𝑑reachd\_{\mathrm{reach}}italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_reach end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ensures that rD⁢(dH)=0subscript𝑟Dsubscript𝑑H0r\_{\mathrm{D}}(d\_{\mathrm{H}})=0italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_D end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_d start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_H end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = 0 for distances beyond the robot’s physical reach, as the robot cannot pose any danger to the human outside this range.

Fig. [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#S3.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 3.3.1 Distance-based Hazard Indicator ‣ 3.3 Definition of Hazard Indicators ‣ 3 Heuristics-based Hazard Analysis ‣ Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a Heuristics-based Approach") presents the plot of the function for different values of α𝛼\alphaitalic\_α. It becomes evident that increasing α𝛼\alphaitalic\_α makes the function sensitive to small changes in distance. This means that even minor changes lead to a significant increase in the hazard. To determine an appropriate value for α𝛼\alphaitalic\_α, Eq. ([3](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#S3.E3 "In 3.3.1 Distance-based Hazard Indicator ‣ 3.3 Definition of Hazard Indicators ‣ 3 Heuristics-based Hazard Analysis ‣ Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a Heuristics-based Approach")) is solved using the specified constraints. It is evident that the evaluation of distance-based hazards relies entirely on the robot’s characteristics and operational parameters.

#### 3.3.2 Velocity-based Hazard Indicator

To assess the hazard level based on the robot’s velocity, both its magnitude and direction must be considered. The metric should reflect that an increase in velocity correlates with a higher associated danger, while a decrease in velocity reduces the hazard. Kulić and Croft ([2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib11)) propose a quadratic function to compute a hazard indicator based on the magnitude of velocity, expressed as:

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | rV⁢(v)subscript𝑟V𝑣\displaystyle r\_{\mathrm{V}}(v)italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_v ) | =kV⋅(v−Vmin)2absent⋅subscript𝑘Vsuperscript𝑣subscript𝑉min2\displaystyle=k\_{\mathrm{V}}\cdot(v-V\_{\mathrm{min}})^{2}= italic\_k start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⋅ ( italic\_v - italic\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT |  | (5) |
|  | withkV  withsubscript𝑘V\displaystyle\text{with}\quad k\_{\mathrm{V}}with italic\_k start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT | =1(Vmax−Vmin)2absent1superscriptsubscript𝑉maxsubscript𝑉min2\displaystyle=\frac{1}{(V\_{\mathrm{max}}-V\_{\mathrm{min}})^{2}}= divide start\_ARG 1 end\_ARG start\_ARG ( italic\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_max end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end\_ARG |  |

Vminsubscript𝑉minV\_{\mathrm{min}}italic\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_min end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the lowest threshold velocity below which the associated risk is negligible. It is typically set to 250250250250mm/smms\mathrm{m}\mathrm{m}\mathrm{/}{\mathrm{s}}roman\_mm / roman\_s (ISO 10218-2, [2011](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib7)). Vmaxsubscript𝑉maxV\_{\mathrm{max}}italic\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_max end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT is chosen in accordance with ISO/TS 15066:2016:15066201615066\mathrm{:}201615066 : 2016.

However, this equation has a limitation. It only partially considers the vector nature of velocity. The directional component is simplified as positive or negative (Kulić and Croft, [2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib11)). To assess danger more accurately, the full directional information must be accounted for, as velocity is not always strictly forward, or backward. Marvel and Norcross ([2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#bib.bib12)) highlight that current standards do not factor in the direction of travel. From a safety perspective, it is assumed that the operator’s movements may follow a worst-case scenario, where the operator moves in the direction of the robot at any moment. To address this, a worst-case-direction vector, d→→𝑑\vec{d}over→ start\_ARG italic\_d end\_ARG, is defined representing the direction in which the hazard is expected to increase the most. This is the case when the robot moves directly toward a human operator. The directional component of the robot’s velocity vector v→→𝑣\vec{v}over→ start\_ARG italic\_v end\_ARG in the direction of d→→𝑑\vec{d}over→ start\_ARG italic\_d end\_ARG is computed by projecting the vectors onto each other. Using the relationship given by the dot product and reordering yields:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | cos⁡(θ)=v→⋅d→‖v→‖⋅‖d→‖𝜃⋅→𝑣→𝑑⋅norm→𝑣norm→𝑑\cos(\theta)=\frac{\vec{v}\cdot\vec{d}}{\|\vec{v}\|\cdot\|\vec{d}\|}roman\_cos ( italic\_θ ) = divide start\_ARG over→ start\_ARG italic\_v end\_ARG ⋅ over→ start\_ARG italic\_d end\_ARG end\_ARG start\_ARG ∥ over→ start\_ARG italic\_v end\_ARG ∥ ⋅ ∥ over→ start\_ARG italic\_d end\_ARG ∥ end\_ARG |  | (6) |

The term represents the directional influence, indicating how much of v→→𝑣\vec{v}over→ start\_ARG italic\_v end\_ARG is aligned with d→→𝑑\vec{d}over→ start\_ARG italic\_d end\_ARG. When c⁢o⁢s⁢(θ)≈1𝑐𝑜𝑠𝜃1cos(\theta)\approx 1italic\_c italic\_o italic\_s ( italic\_θ ) ≈ 1 , the robot’s velocity is highly aligned with the worst-case direction, increasing the hazard. Conversely, when
c⁢o⁢s⁢(θ)≈0𝑐𝑜𝑠𝜃0cos(\theta)\approx 0italic\_c italic\_o italic\_s ( italic\_θ ) ≈ 0 or negative, the robot’s velocity is less aligned or even moving away from the worst-case direction, resulting in a lower hazard indicator. To normalize this directional influence and ensure the risk metric remains within a bounded range of [0,1]01[0,1][ 0 , 1 ], the following equation is used:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | rVdirectional⁢(θ)=1+c⁢o⁢s⁢(θ)2subscript𝑟subscriptVdirectional𝜃1𝑐𝑜𝑠𝜃2r\_{\mathrm{V\_{directional}}}(\theta)=\frac{1+cos(\theta)}{2}italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_directional end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_θ ) = divide start\_ARG 1 + italic\_c italic\_o italic\_s ( italic\_θ ) end\_ARG start\_ARG 2 end\_ARG |  | (7) |

The final velocity-based hazard indicator combines the magnitude of the velocity and its directional influence into a weighted sum.

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | rV⁢(v,θ)=β⋅rV⁢(v)+(1−β)⋅rVdirectional⁢(θ)subscript𝑟V𝑣𝜃⋅𝛽subscript𝑟V𝑣⋅1𝛽subscript𝑟subscriptVdirectional𝜃r\_{\mathrm{V}}(v,\theta)=\beta\cdot r\_{\mathrm{V}}(v)+(1-\beta)\cdot r\_{% \mathrm{V\_{directional}}}(\theta)italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_v , italic\_θ ) = italic\_β ⋅ italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_v ) + ( 1 - italic\_β ) ⋅ italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_directional end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_θ ) |  | (8) |

β𝛽\betaitalic\_β is the weighting factor the range of [0,1]01[0,1][ 0 , 1 ]. The initial estimate for β=3/4𝛽34\beta=3/4italic\_β = 3 / 4 emphasizing the velocity magnitude. The velocity hazard indicator remains zero, if v<V⁢min𝑣𝑉minv<V\mathrm{min}italic\_v < italic\_V roman\_min.

Figure [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#S3.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ 3.3.2 Velocity-based Hazard Indicator ‣ 3.3 Definition of Hazard Indicators ‣ 3 Heuristics-based Hazard Analysis ‣ Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a Heuristics-based Approach") illustrates the hazard indicator as a heat map, plotted based on velocity magnitude and the directional component, represented by the angle θ𝜃\thetaitalic\_θ. The maximum velocity in this plot is set to Vmax=1subscript𝑉max1V\_{\mathrm{max}}=1italic\_V start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_max end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1 m/sms\mathrm{m}\mathrm{/}{\mathrm{s}}roman\_m / roman\_s. As expected, the hazard is highest when the velocity reaches its maximum value and θ=0⁢°𝜃0degree\theta=0$\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$italic\_θ = 0 °, as indicated by the red region on the heat map. Conversely, the hazard is lowest when the velocity is minimal and θ=180⁢°𝜃180degree\theta=180$\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$italic\_θ = 180 °, represented by the green region. Interestingly, the region where the velocity is maximal but indicating the robot is moving away from the human still shows high danger. This is due to the inherent dangers of high-velocity movement, such as the potential for unforeseen circumstances. For example, a robot moving rapidly might accidentally drop an object held by its gripper.

#### 3.3.3 Position of Human Head-based Hazard Indicator

The PHH indicator is unique among the proposed hazard indicators as it is entirely anthropocentric and not derived from established standards. It quantifies the deviation of the human head’s orientation from an expected reference. Similarly to the velocity-based metric, it is designed to increase with larger deviations in orientation and decrease with smaller deviations. PHH is an angular value and is described through a sigmoid growth curve:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | rPHH⁢(P⁢H⁢H)=1−e−(P⁢H⁢Hc)2subscript𝑟PHH𝑃𝐻𝐻1superscript𝑒superscript𝑃𝐻𝐻𝑐2r\_{\mathrm{PHH}}(PHH)=1-e^{-(\frac{PHH}{c})^{2}}italic\_r start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_PHH end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_P italic\_H italic\_H ) = 1 - italic\_e start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - ( divide start\_ARG italic\_P italic\_H italic\_H end\_ARG start\_ARG italic\_c end\_ARG ) start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT |  | (9) |

c𝑐citalic\_c controls the steepness of the curve, enabling fine-tuning to match the desired hazard scaling. To compute the indicator, a reference vector, pref→→subscript𝑝ref\vec{p\_{\mathrm{ref}}}over→ start\_ARG italic\_p start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_ref end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT end\_ARG, is defined, extending from a point on the human head to the robot’s end-effector. This vector represents the ideal direction for human focus during interaction. A current orientation vector, porient→→subscript𝑝orient\vec{p\_{\mathrm{orient}}}over→ start\_ARG italic\_p start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_orient end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT end\_ARG, denoting the actual direction of the human’s gaze, is also constructed. The normalized current orientation vector is projected onto the normalized reference vector. The relationship between the vectors is identical to the one described in Eq. ([6](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#S3.E6 "In 3.3.2 Velocity-based Hazard Indicator ‣ 3.3 Definition of Hazard Indicators ‣ 3 Heuristics-based Hazard Analysis ‣ Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a Heuristics-based Approach")). PHH is compured only for the yaw angle, i.e rotation around the z-axis, as it most critically affects the human’s field of view. However, the other two rotational axes can also be monitored separately using the same function.

When the human operator directly faces the robot P⁢H⁢H=0 °𝑃𝐻𝐻times0degreePHH=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$italic\_P italic\_H italic\_H = start\_ARG 0 end\_ARG start\_ARG times end\_ARG start\_ARG ° end\_ARG, and the hazard is minimal. As the head rotates away, the hazard increases, maxing out at P⁢H⁢H=1𝑃𝐻𝐻1PHH=1italic\_P italic\_H italic\_H = 1 at an angle of 90⁢°90degree90$\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$90 ° meaning the heads orientation is perpendicular to the robot. For angles beyond 90⁢°90degree90$\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$90 °, up to 180⁢°180degree180$\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$180 °, remains one, indicating the operator cannot observe the robot. Beyond this point, the angle is irrelevant, as the situation is considered unsafe. Ultimately, the function ensures rapid convergence to the maximum value beyond a critical angle.

The hazard indicator is illustrated in Fig. [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08316v1#S3.F3 "Figure 3 ‣ 3.3.3 Position of Human Head-based Hazard Indicator ‣ 3.3 Definition of Hazard Indicators ‣ 3 Heuristics-based Hazard Analysis ‣ Dynamic Risk Assessment for Human-Robot Collaboration Using a Heuristics-based Approach") for different values of c𝑐citalic\_c. Adjusting c𝑐citalic\_c is critical to ensure the hazard evaluation aligns with safety requirements and practical expectations, balancing sensitivity and usability.

#### 3.3.4 Total Hazard Indicator

The total hazard indicator is computed, by taking the weighted sum of all considered indicators, and dividing it by the sum of the corresponding weights. The corresponding equation is:

Occupational health and safety issues in human-robot collaboration: State of the art and open challenges
========================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Antonio Giallanza a, Giada La Scalia b, Rosa Micale a, Concetta Manuela La Fata b

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Designing and implementing safe and ergonomic solutions. 
*   •Current trends of HRC-related articles by topics and application areas. 
*   •Overview on Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) concerns in collaborative environments. 
*   •Future lines of research to transfer collaborative robotics from the lab scale to the shop floor. 

Abstract
--------

Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) refers to the interaction of workers and robots in a shared workspace. Owing to the integration of the industrial automation strengths with the inimitable cognitive capabilities of humans, HRC is paramount to move towards advanced and sustainable production systems. Although the overall safety of collaborative robotics has increased over time, further research efforts are needed to allow humans to operate alongside robots, with awareness and trust. Numerous safety concerns are open, and either new or enhanced technical, procedural and organizational measures have to be investigated to design and implement inherently safe and ergonomic automation solutions, aligning the systems performance and the human safety. Therefore, a bibliometric analysis and a literature review are carried out in the present paper to provide a comprehensive overview of Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) issues in HRC. As a result, the most researched topics and application areas, and the possible future lines of research are identified. Reviewed articles stress the central role played by humans during collaboration, underlining the need to integrate the human factor in the hazard analysis and risk assessment. Human-centered design and cognitive engineering principles also require further investigations to increase the worker acceptance and trust during collaboration. Deepened studies are compulsory in the healthcare sector, to investigate the social and ethical implications of HRC. Whatever the application context is, the implementation of more and more advanced technologies is fundamental to overcome the current HRC safety concerns, designing low-risk HRC systems while ensuring the system productivity.

SUMMARY:
It developed safety risk ratings for critical hazards in three categories of RA technologies (wearable robots, remote operated robots, and onsite automated

FULL CONTENT:
KEY FINDINGS FROM RESEARCH WWW.CPWR.COM • Safety Data Sheets on the use of wearable robots (exoskeletons), remote-operated robots (e.g., drones and unmanned ground vehicles [UGVs]), and onsite automated robots (e.g., bricklaying robots).
• Job Hazard Analysis protocols for three tasks: drywall installation, bricklaying, and concrete grinding and polishing. It also developed practical assessment manual containing: • Descriptions of available RA technologies.
• Applications of RA technologies.
• Factors that influence the use of RA technologies.
• Current standards and procedures for RA.
Overview Robotics and automation (RA)—such as exoskeletons, drones, and single-task construction robots—offer new possibilities to combat challenges facing the construction trades, including unsafe and dangerous working conditions. Despite RA’s potential positive impacts, human-robot interaction (HRI) can also introduce new hazards on the job site and elevate the potential impact of existing hazards. While some studies have assessed the effectiveness of RA technologies, none have evaluated the safety risks associated with implementing RA in the construction industry, and there is no HRI safety risk assessment protocol to assist practitioners conducting pre-task planning that involves RA. This study developed a practical process and tools for practitioners to identify and quantify HRI safety risks when using RA in construction operations. For more information, contact: Chukwuma Nnaji: cnnaji@eng.ua.edu Read the report https://bit.ly/3oaijUs ©2021, CPWR-The Center for Construction Research and Training. All rights reserved. CPWR is the research and training arm of NABTU. Production of this document was supported by cooperative agreement OH 009762 from the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH). The contents are solely the responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily represent the official views of NIOSH.
Assessing Safety Risks of Human-Robot Interaction Occupational Safety and Health Protocol for Assessing Human-Robot Interaction Safety Risks Chukwuma Nnaji, John Gambatese, and Ifeanyi Okpala. CPWR Small Study, 2021 Key Findings The study identified 40 hazards associated with the use of robotics and automation (RA) for construction operations, classifying them into seven groups: Human; Control; Unauthorized Access or Operational Situation Awareness; Mechanical Concerns; Environmental Sources; Power Systems; and Improper Installation. It developed safety risk ratings for critical hazards in three categories of RA technologies (wearable robots, remote operated robots, and onsite automated robots) when used for three construction tasks (bricklaying, concrete grinding and polishing, and drywall installation). It identified 22 preventive strategies for mitigating human-robot interaction (HRI) safety risks during construction projects. It created a practical process for assessing and controlling HRI safety risk that comprises:

enriched_points.expanded_content: Human-robot collaboration involves intricate interactions influenced by unpredictable human behavior, varying task dynamics, and environmental factors, complicating risk assessment processes.
Traditional risk assessment methods often rely on static models and expert judgment, which may not adequately address the dynamic nature of human-robot interactions.
Dynamic risk assessments are essential to adapt to real-time changes in collaborative environments, ensuring safety standards evolve alongside operational conditions.
